skill.name: dotnet-backend-patterns
skill.description: Guides C# and .NET backend development with practical patterns for APIs, services, repositories, caching, configuration, and testing.
skill.summary: Apply practical .NET backend patterns for APIs, data access, caching, and tests.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found Markdown code fences, C# examples, and ordinary .NET identifiers misclassified as shell execution, reconnaissance, or weak cryptography. No prompt injection, malware intent, credential exfiltration, install scripts, or hidden executable behavior was found. The only real caution is an intentionally unsafe SQL example that is clearly labeled as an anti-pattern and followed by a parameterized replacement.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown code fences around C# examples. They do not execute commands, launch processes, or request shell access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown fences and C# snippets. I found no shell command invocation or executable script behavior at those locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are C# and Documentation Terms
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts point to words such as .NET, Design, Split Queries, Includes, and JsonSerializer examples. They are not MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic algorithm usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced code and headings contain query specification properties, JSON serialization examples, and documentation text. No cryptographic API or hashing algorithm is used there.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance and Hidden File Alerts Are Benign .NET Text
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance and hidden file alerts are caused by ordinary C# names, documentation text, and the .NET term in the skill description. They do not inspect the host system or access hidden files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are educational C# database and async examples. I found no file enumeration, environment probing, or hidden path access behavior.

low_findings.title: Labeled SQL Injection Anti-Pattern Included for Teaching
low_findings.description: The Dapper reference includes a string-interpolated SQL query as a bad example, immediately followed by a parameterized query. This is safe as documentation, but users should not copy the bad example into production code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The unsafe SQL pattern is present, but it is explicitly labeled as bad and paired with a safe replacement. The risk is educational misuse, not malicious package behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Do Not Copy String-Interpolated SQL Anti-Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: A SQL injection example appears only as a documented anti-pattern. Users should follow the parameterized query shown next to it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky pattern is real, but the context clearly marks it as unsafe. It does not indicate malicious intent in the skill.

content.value_statement: Modern .NET backends require consistent patterns for data access, dependency injection, caching, and tests. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical guidance for designing and reviewing production C# services.
actual_capabilities: Explains clean architecture project structure for .NET services and APIs.
Shows dependency injection lifetimes, options binding, and service registration patterns.
Covers async and cancellation patterns for backend request flows.
Compares EF Core and Dapper examples for repository and query design.
Provides caching, resilience, and testing examples for production services.
limitations: Does not run migrations, generate projects, or execute .NET commands by itself.
Examples need adaptation to the target domain model and database provider.
Does not replace security review for authentication, authorization, or secrets handling.
Some snippets are templates and may require missing application types.

use_cases.title: Design a New API Backend
use_cases.description: Plan service layers, repository boundaries, dependency injection, and configuration before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Review Existing C# Services
use_cases.description: Check async usage, data access patterns, caching behavior, and test coverage against practical .NET conventions.

use_cases.title: Choose EF Core or Dapper Patterns
use_cases.description: Compare query styles and repository examples when balancing domain modeling, performance, and maintainability.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how to structure a small .NET Web API using the patterns in this skill. Include services, repositories, configuration, and tests.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this C# service for async, dependency injection, validation, and data access issues. Suggest focused improvements using this skill.

prompt_templates.title: Design Data Access
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a data access approach for this use case. Compare EF Core and Dapper, then recommend repository methods, query patterns, and tests.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor for Production
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this .NET backend design for production readiness. Address caching, resilience, cancellation, logging, configuration, and integration tests.

output_examples.input: Review a product repository that mixes synchronous calls with async methods.
output: A prioritized review of blocking calls and cancellation gaps.
A safer repository shape using async methods consistently.
Testing notes for success, not found, and cancellation paths.

output_examples.input: Design a data layer for search-heavy product catalog queries.
output: A recommendation for Dapper read paths and EF Core write paths.
Guidance for parameterized filters, pagination, and indexes.
A short list of tradeoffs to validate with the team.

output_examples.input: Improve caching for a frequently requested API endpoint.
output: A cache key and invalidation strategy.

best_practices: Start with the existing application architecture and adapt examples to local naming and boundaries.
Use parameterized queries, cancellation tokens, and typed configuration in production code.
Ask for tests with each generated service, repository, or endpoint change.
anti_patterns: Do not copy template snippets without validating domain types, lifetimes, and database behavior.
Do not use string interpolation for user-controlled SQL values.
Do not treat caching examples as complete authorization or data freshness designs.

faq.question: Does this skill execute .NET commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Any build, test, or migration command must be run separately by the user or agent.

faq.question: Can it help with both EF Core and Dapper?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes reference patterns for EF Core repositories, Dapper queries, transactions, and performance choices.

faq.question: Is this useful for minimal APIs?
faq.answer: Yes. The service, configuration, validation, and data access patterns apply to controllers and minimal API endpoints.

faq.question: Does it cover authentication and authorization?
faq.answer: Only indirectly. Security-sensitive identity design still needs separate review and project-specific requirements.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this for code review?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill gives review criteria for async code, dependency injection, repositories, caching, and tests.

faq.question: Are the snippets production ready?
faq.answer: They are templates and examples. They should be adapted, tested, and reviewed before production use.
